a: Production-and-nonsupervisory status is not identical to physical task content.
a: The frozen compensation ratio is ancestor-level 23812 and mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts before harmonization.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Structural Steel and Precast Concrete Contractors

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based specialty subcontracting, generally awarded as fixed-price, lump-sum, or unit-price scopes with progress billing and change orders; repeat revenue comes through relationships with general contractors, fabricators, precast suppliers, industrial owners, and public agencies rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Shells, captive erection crews, non-control financing vehicles, pure steel fabrication or precast manufacturing without field contracting operations, poured-in-place concrete contractors, and engineering-only firms.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors that erect and assemble structural steel or precast concrete and install reinforcing steel for external owners, general contractors, fabricators, and precast producers, including new work, additions, maintenance, and repairs.

a.bridge: BLS CES measures production-and-nonsupervisory headcount rather than occupation-level wages or AI task exposure. Its May 2026 table shows 72,300 production and nonsupervisory employees against 85,000 total employees for NAICS 23812, indicating a predominantly field workforce. I assign low exposure to rigging, connecting, bolting, welding, reinforcing-steel placement, and precast erection, but higher exposure to estimating, model review, lift and logistics planning support, submittals, RFIs, scheduling, safety documentation, and administration. This can understate computer-vision and model-based automation, while overstating substitution where incomplete models and safety-critical judgment require review.
caveats: CES uses the five-digit 23812 series and does not provide a full wage-weighted occupation matrix for this packet.
Production-and-nonsupervisory status is not identical to physical task content.
The frozen compensation ratio is ancestor-level 23812 and mixes 2024 wages with 2022 receipts before harmonization.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across at least 20 steel erectors, rebar placers, and precast installers that separately measures office, VDC, supervision, and craft tasks and tests AI-enabled hours saved would materially move the estimate.
a.rationale: Most payroll supports physical erection: ironworkers work outside, at height, and perform dangerous, physically demanding tasks. The addressable slice is concentrated in better-paid preconstruction, project-management, VDC, documentation, and back-office work. AISC describes BIM as an everyday occurrence in structural steel and provides steel-specific model-review guidance, which creates structured digital inputs that can expand AI assistance beyond generic clerical work, but not replace site execution.

d5.bridge: BLS does not publish a separate 2024–2034 projection line for NAICS 238120 in its industry table, so I use the broader foundation, structure, and building exterior contractor projection of 4.0% employment growth and the 4% ironworker employment projection. Halving the ten-year growth horizon supports about 1.02 over five years. Employment is not constant-price, constant-quality service demand, and productivity, union work rules, offsite assembly, and subcontracting can separate them in either direction.
caveats: Employment is an indirect demand proxy and the available projection is broad-industry or occupation level rather than NAICS 238120 output.
Census construction spending is nominal and short-term, not the required constant-price quantity measure.
Demand varies materially between structural steel, rebar placement, and precast erection and across local end markets.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year Dodge Construction Network or ConstructConnect forecasts of inflation-adjusted steel tonnage, precast volume, and reinforcing-steel placement by end market, weighted to target contractors' revenue mix, would materially narrow this range.
d5.rationale: Steel, reinforcing steel, and precast remain important to commercial and industrial buildings and to bridge repair and replacement; BLS expects these uses to support ironworker demand. PCI's 2026 forecast indicates total construction growth through 2029 but at slower rates, with strength in data centers, healthcare, office, hotels, warehouses, K-12, and bridges offset by weakness in multifamily and some regions. Current construction spending is softer than a year earlier, justifying a broad cyclical range.

caveats: Eligibility is not observed, and the NAICS combines structural-steel erection, reinforcing-steel placement, and precast installation.
The supplied firm count is a margin bridge from size-class data rather than an observed list of firms in the EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level roster matched to state contractor licenses, AISC certification records, union agreements, bonding capacity, customer concentration, websites, and owner-role interviews would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The industry definition is an outsourced field-installation activity, and Census reports 4,005 employer establishments in 2023, so most operating firms in the supplied LMM count fit the lens. Ineligibility arises where operations are captive to a fabricator or manufacturer, the firm is misclassified, licenses or bonding cannot transfer, customer concentration is extreme, union relationships or key-person credentials are fragile, or the business lacks management independent of its owner.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 23812 ($6,980,900,135) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 23812 ($20,592,128,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 23812. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0796 (margins.json[23]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Engineering/Construction EBITDA/Sales 7.96%); revenue/employee $259,108 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 238120); class <5: 1,649 firms, avg revenue/firm $532,972 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $42,425 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 689 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,458,467 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $116,094 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 648 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,294,228 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $262,221 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 757 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,679,078 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $850,055 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 127 firms, avg revenue/firm $45,265,323 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,603,120 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $109,638,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,727,201 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 152

caveats: Operator requirement is a five-year judgment rather than a directly measured outcome.
Integrated fabricator-erector models and modular construction may advance faster in selected segments than nationally.
o.change_evidence: Procurement data from major general contractors, fabricators, and precast producers showing the share of erection scopes moved to self-perform, automated installation, or integrated design-manufacture-install offerings would change the estimate.
o.rationale: The year-five service still requires an accountable contractor to plan and execute crane picks, rigging, connecting, bolting, welding, reinforcement placement, temporary stability, access, weather decisions, inspection coordination, schedule, and safety. BLS emphasizes that ironworkers operate at height in dangerous physical conditions. AI and BIM improve coordination but do not assume field safety, contractual, quality, or warranty accountability. Vertical integration by fabricators or precast suppliers and greater modular self-perform account for the small reduction from one.

caveats: No direct study of AI-benefit pass-through in steel erection, reinforcing-steel placement, or precast installation was found.
Retention differs across hard-bid public work, negotiated industrial projects, and subcontractor relationships with affiliated fabricators.
q.change_evidence: Matched job-level estimates, bid spreads, actual labor, change orders, rework, and margin data from AI adopters and non-adopters, plus customer interviews on rebid pricing, would directly measure retention rather than gross productivity.
q.rationale: AI-enabled savings on awarded fixed-price or unit-price jobs can initially remain with the contractor, particularly when they prevent estimating misses, rework, coordination delays, or unfilled overhead hiring. Frequent project rebidding, sophisticated general contractors and fabricators, transparent labor units, and low-bid procurement pass much of a generic cost advantage into future prices. Relationship work, scarce erection capacity, safety performance, and proprietary production history permit some durable retention.

caveats: RICS respondents were global construction professionals, not a US NAICS 238120 LMM sample.
No controlled concrete-or-steel-erector implementation study was found.
rho.change_evidence: A vendor-neutral longitudinal cohort of US steel and precast erectors reporting sustained usage, review burden, error rates, workflow integration, and paid hours eliminated for estimating, VDC, project controls, and accounting would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Structured BIM/VDC workflows, recurring submittal and RFI patterns, and severe hiring difficulty support implementation of AI assistance within five years. Against that, RICS found 45% of surveyed construction organizations had no AI use and only 1% had scaled across projects, while skills, integration, and data quality were common barriers. Field connectivity, model completeness, interoperability among detailing and project systems, union work rules, quality assurance, and safety liability constrain unattended implementation.

s5.bridge: FMI/CFMA surveyed nearly 300 leaders across engineering and construction rather than this six-digit LMM population; 42% of the underlying study respondents were trade contractors/subcontractors, and 38% of owners planned to exit in three to five years. I discount planned exits for internal, partial, delayed, failed, and non-transferable outcomes. Larger and more transition-aware respondents may cause an upward bias, while aging owners in smaller specialty contractors may cause the proxy to understate pressure.
caveats: FMI/CFMA is a broad voluntary E&C survey and not a probability sample of steel and precast erection contractors.
Internal equity transitions do not necessarily constitute a qualifying control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Completed-control data from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, AISC member transactions, state contractor-license changes, surety records, and ESOP filings matched to target-band firms over five years would materially narrow the estimate.
s5.rationale: Succession pressure is material, but qualifying control transfers are only a subset of owner exits. FMI reports about 64% intend internal ownership transitions, and 51% of owners planning to exit within five years lack a defined strategy. Financing, surety support, key leadership, certifications, safety history, and labor relationships can lengthen or prevent a transfer.

principal_driver: Digitally modeled but physically executed work creates useful AI-assisted coordination while preserving the need for a safety-accountable field operator.
principal_weakness: Most labor remains hazardous hands-on erection, and hard-bid project turnover exposes savings to customer and competitor repricing.
